topic: Changing Relations: Northern Ireland & its Neighbours 1965-98
subject: history

items.q: What did the civil rights movement of the late 1960s actually demand?
items.correct: Equal treatment for all citizens within Northern Ireland — fair housing allocation, an end to gerrymandering and 'one man, one vote' in local elections.
items.misconception: NICRA was simply a front organisation demanding an immediate united Ireland.
items.random_wrong: NICRA campaigned mainly for women's right to vote in Westminster elections.
items.why: Students read later constitutional conflict back into 1968; the original demands were about equal citizenship, which is why unionist claims it was an IRA front are debated.

items.q: What happened on Bloody Sunday in 1972?
items.correct: British soldiers shot dead thirteen civil rights marchers in Derry/Londonderry on 30 January 1972; a fourteenth victim died later.
items.misconception: Bloody Sunday was the IRA bombing of Belfast city centre.
items.random_wrong: Bloody Sunday was a gun battle between rival paramilitary groups in Dublin.
items.why: Students confuse Bloody Sunday (January 1972, army shootings in Derry) with Bloody Friday (July 1972, IRA bombs in Belfast).

items.q: Did the Good Friday Agreement instantly end all violence?
items.correct: It created the political framework that largely ended the conflict, but violence did not vanish — the Omagh bomb came in August 1998 and decommissioning disputes dragged on for years.
items.misconception: The 1998 Agreement immediately stopped all paramilitary activity.
items.random_wrong: The Agreement removed Northern Ireland from the United Kingdom.
items.why: Students treat 1998 as a clean endpoint; the peace process continued well beyond the Agreement itself.

items.q: What did the Good Friday Agreement of 1998 establish?
items.correct: A power-sharing Assembly and the principle that Northern Ireland's status can only change by consent
items.why: It's A power-sharing Assembly and the principle that Northern Ireland's status can only change by consent. “Immediate Irish unification” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What happened on Bloody Sunday, 30 January 1972?
items.correct: British paratroopers shot dead thirteen civil rights marchers in Derry/Londonderry
items.misconception: The IRA bombed Belfast city centre
items.random_wrong: The Good Friday Agreement was signed
items.why: Correct answer: British paratroopers shot dead thirteen civil rights marchers in Derry/Londonderry. “The IRA bombed Belfast city centre” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did NICRA campaign for from 1967?
items.correct: Equal rights in housing, jobs and local government voting within Northern Ireland
items.random_wrong: The closure of the border with the Republic
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Equal rights in housing, jobs and local government voting within Northern Ireland. “An immediate united Ireland” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why did unionists protest against the Anglo-Irish Agreement of 1985?
items.correct: It gave the Dublin government a consultative role in Northern Ireland without unionist consent
items.misconception: It abolished the Northern Ireland border immediately
items.random_wrong: It banned all unionist political parties
items.why: The answer examiners want is: It gave the Dublin government a consultative role in Northern Ireland without unionist consent. “It abolished the Northern Ireland border immediately” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Good Friday Agreement signed?
items.why: The real figure is 1998 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
